title: Nona's Venture
html:
TL;DR

  • December 2020 product-spec doc for AwakenCollectionShop — a curated quarantine self-care gift box business
  • Three SKU variations built around: book ($1.50–4), mug ($0.79–0.99), crystal ($2), candle, note, and tea — packaged for $5–7 cost, retail TBD
  • Brand keyword cluster: "Quarantine, Self Care, Spiritual, Surprise, Mystery, Earthy, Sustainable, Provisions, Intention, Awakening" — pure pandemic-era vibe arbitrage
  • Owned by "Nona" (likely a friend/sibling collaborator — confirm) but Yahir is doing the cost-modeling work — same gift-box mechanic he later dismisses in the Valeria critique
  • Shows Yahir's earliest documented unit-economics thinking: line-item COGS, packaging, before-margin pricing — the muscle that later powers bromenu and rankett

Notes

A short dec-2020 spec sheet — Yahir cost-modeling a small ecommerce gift-box concept being launched (likely) by someone named Nona. Three bundle variations, each anchored on a $1.50–4 book and $0.79–0.99 mug, dressed with crystals, a candle, a handwritten note, and tea. Packaging budget $3–5. Total component cost lands around $7–10 per box.

The keyword cluster is a perfect snapshot of the 2020 pandemic DTC moment — "quarantine," "self-care," "spiritual," "earthy," "sustainable" — the same aesthetic vocabulary that later shows up in Té Amo's herbalism positioning and would be ridiculed by Yahir himself in his critique to Valeria three years later as "good vibes" without bread-and-butter revenue logic.

This is also the earliest doc in the vault where Yahir builds a per-SKU COGS table — the analytical muscle that recurs in bromenu and rankett business modeling.

Open question: who is "Nona"? Possibly grandmother (abuela/Nona)? Possibly a friend's project Yahir was advising? No second mention in the vault yet.

related.label: Té Amo — Tea and Art House
